title: git + quarto
text:
Use git and github

Download repositories (online project folder)
Track your changes (backup + undo)
Develop code collaboratively
Host codebase + documentation website (ex: runway)
Installation instructions

text:
Clone a repo
VScode: guide
RStudio:

Keep exactly the github repo name as Project directory name!

title: git + quarto
section: Track your changes in small batches
text:
Track your changes in small batches

In a shared repo, Pull before you work.
Click Commit to select files (or parts of them) for one unit of change.

Write a short informative message (why, not what) and press Commit.
Push your commits to the github online repo.

title: git + quarto
text:
Quarto documents

Mix code and markdown text
Hassle-free and reproducible reports / presentations / websites
One main file with the single source of truth (SSOT)
Full publishing ecosystem, see quarto.org
R and Python chunks can both be included (and hand off data to each other)
Many settings are available! (see quarto.org)
In RStudio, click File → New File → Quarto Document
In VScode, install the quarto extension, create a .qmd file — for a template, copy content e.g. from the quarto guide

title: git + quarto
text: Quarto reports

visual editor

title: git + quarto
text:
Quarto tips & tricks

RStudio Cog button settings:

preview in Viewer pane
chunk output in console

Name your chunks for rendering progress, debugging, figure file names, cache names
Use code folding :)
More ideas in the homework

title: git + quarto
text:
Sharing documents/presentations
In order of complexity / effort:

Simple qmd file in some repo: qmd → html → rendered (alt)
Readme file in dedicated github repo: qmd → readme
Simple website with short URL: qmd → brry.github.io/ice
Complex website: qmd → brry.github.io/rdwd

title: git + quarto
text:
Homework

implement suggested changes in 4_practice.qmd

title: git + quarto
section: Summary: use version control and quarto markdown documents
text:
Summary: use version control and quarto markdown documents

Copy github repo URL
Clone (download):

VScode: Source Control → Clone Repository
RStudio: create project (version control)

Work, commit, repeat
Pull (if in a team)
Push (upload)
qmd: one single main file with text and code
Code chunks in any programming language
Can be compiled to html, pdf, website
Homework: make changes in the qmd file

← DE4W exercises overview

Software installation guide
for ‘Data engineering for wearables’
based on instructions for Fundamentals of Programming
For data engineering pipelines, you have a lot of software to install.
It’s a bit much at once, but it will be worth it!
Any improvements to this guide are very welcome!
Jump to R, Git, Python, VScode, qmd, Misc

R

Step 1a: install R + RStudio, change the Rdata settings
Step 1b: check if R is on the PATH and has a version > 4.3.x.



Git

Step 2a: in a sensible (easy to reach) location on your computer, create a folder for this course. Use a short expressive name like DataEngineeringWearables. Do not use spaces in file/folder names, see good practices.
Step 2b: install git, including the SSH key setup.
If you already have a personal github account, use that :)
Step 2c: follow the use git section to download this repo into your main course folder (step 2a).
Step 2d: run install/2d_install_Rpackages.R. Tip: Use the Files pane (RStudio bottom right) to select and open the file.

From now on, keyboard shortcuts with CTRL mean CMD on MacOS.


Python

Step 3a: check if Python is installed:

in a terminal (see step 1b), type python
if found with a version 3.9+, type quit or quit() and proceed to step 3c.
if not found, open the file install/3a_check_python_installed.py with any IDE.
run it as instructed inside.
see if you get a printout with a version > 3.9.
if yes, skip step 3b. You do NOT want several versions of Python installed.

Step 3b: if not yet installed, install Python with one of

standalone installation: Python, hints on Windows
installation via R (way easier, especially on Mac): run install/3b_install_python.R line by line

Step 3c: install Python modules:

if installed via reticulate, in an R console (e.g. in RStudio), run
reticulate::py_install(c("numpy", "pandas", "matplotlib"))
else, run in the terminal (step 1b):
pip install numpy pandas matplotlib (pip3 on Mac)

Step 3d: run install/3d_check_python_setup.py



VScode
On Berry’s behalf, this step is optional. I will demo everything in RStudio.

Step 4a: install VScode, then

in the Explorer Tab CTRL + SHIFT + E, open the main course folder from step 2a.
when prompted: manage restricted mode - add folder, click “trust”
open the settings (CTRL + ,), search “workbench editor enable preview” and uncheck the box

Step 4b: disable telemetry if you don’t want to send data to Micro$oft:

open the Settings (CTRL + ,), search “Telemetry level”, set to “off”
open the Command Palette (CTRL + SHIFT + P), search “Configure Runtime Arguments” and in the argv.json file, change the value for “enable-crash-reporter” to false, restart VScode

Step 4c: run R scripts in VScode:

open the file install/4c_set_cran_mirror.R (Explorer Tab CTRL + SHIFT + E)
install the R extension when prompted (or manually under Extensions with CTRL + SHIFT + X)
run the file and (as instructed there), edit and save the .Rprofile file

Step 4d: run Python scripts in VScode:

open install/3d_check_python_setup.py (don’t run it yet)
install the Python extension when prompted (or manually)
open the Settings (CTRL + ,), search “execin”, check the box for “Python > Terminal: execute in file dir” (Source)
now run the file as instructed inside.

Step 4e: customize keyboard shortcuts, suggestions as in RStudio.

press CTRL + K + S (or click the cog wheel at the bottom left).
set each wanted shortcut, then rightclick -> “show same keybindings” to deal with duplicates.

search for the action e.g. with “python run” or “comment”








Action
Shortcut




Python: Run Selection/Line in Python Terminal
CTRL + ENTER


Python: Run Python File in Terminal
CTRL + SHIFT + S


Toggle Line Comment andToggle Block Comment
CTRL + SHIFT + C


Delete Line
CTRL + D


Duplicate Selection
CTRL + SHIFT + D






qmd

Step 5a: run quarto markdown scripts in VScode:

open install/5a_check_quarto.qmd
install the Quarto extension when prompted (or manually)
render (a.k.a. compile) the file as instructed inside

Step 5b: open install/5a_check_quarto.qmd in RStudio

render as instructed

Step 5c: open install/5c_check_quarto_python.qmd in RStudio

run code chunks as instructed




Misc

Step 6: housekeeping:

on Windows: in the File explorer, click the “View” tab , then “Show” and check the boxes for “File name extensions” and “Hidden items”
on Mac: in any Finder folder, press CMD + Shift + . to show hidden files


You made it through! 🎉
Now you’re set up for the rest of the course (and beyond).
Again: improvements to this guide are very welcome!

title: check_quarto_python
text:
Testing python chunk execution

library(reticulate)


testing_object = 44


testing_object = testing_object + 11

In RStudio in the following chunk, click on the arrow to “run all chunks above”, then inside the chunk double-click on testing_object to mark it and hit CTRL/CMD + ENTER to see if you can directly see 55.

testing_object

55


If this is not the case, check this issue on Mac for a workaround.

title: check_quarto
text:
This is a mini quarto document with text and some R code.

in RStudio, click “Render” at the top
in VScode, click the Preview button (topright)

This will execute the code chunks and weave together the text, code and output.
In RStudio, I recommend to set (Cog symbol)
- Preview in Viewer Pane
- and (personal preference) Chunk Output in Console.

pairs(iris[, 1:4], main="Anderson's Iris Data",
      col=c("orange", "salmon", "lightblue")[unclass(iris$Species)], 
      pch=16, cex=1.5, lower.panel=NULL, las=1)








More in lesson i.2, specifically the homework.

title: Data engineering for wearables quarto demo
text:
← DE4W exercises overview
Implement the ideas below that seem useful to you. Keep your changes local (don’t push to the online repo).
Further ideas

title: Data engineering for wearables quarto demo
text:
Check package availability

import matplotlib, pandas


if(!requireNamespace("rdwd", quietly=TRUE))
    install.packages("rdwd")
rdwd::updateRdwd()

rdwd is up to date, compared to github.com/brry/rdwd. Version 1.9.12 (2026-03-30)

title: Data engineering for wearables quarto demo
text:
Get weather data
download recent weather data using rdwd

library(rdwd)
link <- selectDWD("Potsdam", res="daily", var="kl", per="recent")
clim <- dataDWD(link, varnames=TRUE, force=24, quiet=TRUE)

title: Data engineering for wearables quarto demo
text:
Visualise recent temperature

plotDWD(clim, "TMK.Lufttemperatur", main=range(clim$MESS_DATUM))

title: Data engineering for wearables quarto demo
text:
Transfer to Python

clim_py = r.clim
print(f"Dataset shape: {clim_py.shape[0]} rows, {clim_py.shape[1]} columns")
clim_py = clim_py.select_dtypes(include=['float64', 'int64'])
clim_py.hist(figsize=(20, 16), bins=5)

title: Data engineering for wearables quarto demo
text:
Summary statistics

# Calculate and display some basic statistics
print("\n=== Summary Statistics for Temperature ===")


=== Summary Statistics for Temperature ===

temp_stats = clim_py['TMK.Lufttemperatur'].describe()
print(temp_stats)

title: DE4W exercise: Software
text:
Why learn Python and R

Languages for data analysis / visualization and statistics
Widely used in research and business
Free, open source (accountable, reproducible, expandable)
Large user community (many methods)
Interpreted language (no compilation)
High-level (readable for humans)
Dynamic typing and binding (data type checked at run-time)
Coding makes your work efficient, productive and replicable


–> Different course formats at brry.github.io/course

title: DE4W exercise: Software
text:
When to use which language

Use what you’re most comfortable with!
Use what your team uses
Choose by strengths (ease of use, choice of packages):

Python: Machine Learning
R: stats / visualisation / data analysis
(both can do all of this)

Choose by programming philosophy:

Python: object-oriented (methods bundled with data)
R: functional (functions return reproducible results)

Choose by implementation details:

Python: informative error messages, systematic function naming
R: easy install, easy REPL, F1 for help, systematic code syntax

title: DE4W exercise: Software
text:
Good coding practices

DRY: Don’t repeat yourself
Do not maintain multiple variants of code!
Duplicate code → function / vectorize / loop
Code should be clear, simple, modular, documented, fast, tested
Many concrete tips at brry.github.io/course/good-practice.html

title: DE4W exercise: Software
text:
